planting.note: Attach offset pups to the rough bark of a host tree or mount on a piece of untreated wood using monofilament or cotton string; do not use wire or metal clips. Giant Wild Pine requires no soil, absorbing water and nutrients through its leaf scales — mist 2–3 times per week in warm months and once per week in winter.

directions: Giant Wild Pine is the largest native bromeliad in the continental United States, native to the cypress swamps, mangrove edges, and tropical hardwood hammocks of south Florida and the Florida Keys, where it grows as a true epiphyte on the branches of cypress, live oak, and other trees, absorbing all its water and nutrients from the air, rain, and debris through specialized scales (trichomes) on its leaves. Its dramatic tank-forming rosette of stiff, gray-green leaves holds a cup of water that serves as a vital micro-habitat for tree frogs, insects, and even small lizards; a tall, multi-colored flower spike of red and purple bracts emerges in spring, followed by the death of the main rosette and production of one or more offset pups that continue the plant's life. Listed as Threatened in Florida due to the invasion of the Mexican Bromeliad Weevil (Metamasius callizona), which has devastated wild populations, Giant Wild Pine is most effectively grown secured to the rough bark of a host tree in bright, indirect light with regular misting to replace the moisture it would receive from tropical rains and morning dew.

planting.note: Attach individual rosettes or clumps to tree branches or mounts using monofilament; Ball Moss naturally seeds itself and colonizes tree bark, utility wires, and fences through wind-dispersed seeds. No soil or watering required once established — it is a true atmospheric bromeliad.

directions: Ball Moss is a native atmospheric bromeliad, native to the southeastern United States, Central America, South America, and the Caribbean, growing as an epiphyte on tree branches, utility wires, and even bare rock and fences in zones 8–11 — the silver-gray, ball-shaped clusters of recurved, scale-covered leaves are a familiar sight on live oaks and other trees throughout the Gulf Coast, Texas, and Florida. Often confused with Spanish moss (Tillandsia usneoides), Ball Moss grows in compact, round clusters rather than draping curtains, and is similarly harmless to its host tree — it uses the tree only for support and draws no nutrients from it, absorbing water and minerals entirely from the air and rain through its silvery trichome-covered leaves. Tiny, inconspicuous lavender flowers appear on slender stalks in late summer, followed by elongated seed capsules that split to release seeds attached to silky hairs (like dandelion seeds) that carry them on the wind to new hosts. Ball Moss requires no care, no soil, and no watering — simply attach a clump to rough bark in a bright location and allow it to grow and self-seed.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or loamy soil in full sun. Florida Royal Palm performs best in moist, humid conditions and benefits from regular irrigation and fertilization during the growing season; it is intolerant of frost below 30°F.

directions: Florida Royal Palm is the most stately and formal of the native Florida palms, endemic to the wet, subtropical forests and swamp edges of the Everglades and extreme southern Florida, where it rises with a dramatically smooth, grayish-green, ringed trunk topped by a brilliant emerald-green crownshaft and an arching crown of long, gracefully curving feather-leaf fronds — a silhouette that defines the classical tropical landscape aesthetic. Once widely planted along formal avenues and boulevards in south Florida and Cuba (where the related Roystonea regia has the same role), Florida Royal Palm is a protected native species in Florida and an important component of Everglades ecosystem restoration. The white to purple flowers attract native bees, and the small olive-like fruits feed a wide variety of native birds and are an important food for the threatened Florida manatee in flooded situations. Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in zone 10–11; fertilize regularly with a palm-specific fertilizer to prevent nutrient deficiencies.

planting.note: Scarify seeds by nicking the seed coat or soaking in hot water (not boiling) for 24 hours before planting; or plant nursery transplants in spring in any well-drained soil in full sun. Royal Poinciana is intolerant of frost, heavy clay soil, and poor drainage.

directions: Royal Poinciana is widely considered the most spectacular flowering tree in the world, native to the dry deciduous forests of Madagascar and now naturalized throughout the tropics and subtropics, producing in late spring and summer an overwhelming canopy of enormous, brilliant scarlet-orange flowers with yellow-tipped petals that can completely conceal the wide-spreading, umbrella-shaped crown — a display so vivid that the tree is visible from miles away. The fine, bipinnate, ferny foliage provides delicate shade during the non-flowering months, and the dark brown seed pods (up to 2 feet long) persist through winter, rattling in the wind. In zones 10–11 it is reliably perennial, and in favorable coastal conditions may be semi-evergreen; it is killed to the ground by even light frost and therefore confined to South Florida, the Florida Keys, and other subtropical areas in the continental US. Grows extremely fast — up to 5 feet per year in good conditions — but needs 5–10 years to reach first flowering. Provide well-drained soil, full sun, and space for its wide-spreading roots and canopy.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in wet to moist, highly acidic (pH 3.5–5.5), sandy or peaty soil in full sun. Atlantic White Cedar is a bog and pocosin specialist that requires constantly wet, highly acidic, low-nutrient soil to thrive; it will not prosper in ordinary garden soils or in neutral to alkaline conditions.

directions: Atlantic White Cedar is a graceful, narrow-crowned native conifer, native to the freshwater wetlands, pocosins, and Atlantic white cedar swamps of the eastern coastal plain from Maine south to Florida and along the Gulf Coast to Mississippi, growing in pure stands in permanently wet, highly acidic, peat-based soils where the combination of constant moisture and extreme acidity prevents most other tree species from competing. Its scaly, aromatic, blue-green foliage on a stately, upright, narrow crown makes it one of the most elegant native conifers, and the wood — naturally resistant to decay and reputed to last for centuries — was historically one of the most valuable woods in colonial America, used for ship building, shingles, and water pipes. The dense, humid microclimate within a cedar swamp supports an extraordinary community of rare and specialized plants and animals, and Atlantic white cedar restoration is a major conservation priority along the coastal plain. Grow in full sun in constantly wet, highly acidic, low-nutrient, sandy or peaty soil; it will not adapt to ordinary garden conditions without mimicking the bog environment it requires.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun. Satinleaf tolerates a range of soil types including marl and sandy soils and is moderately drought-tolerant once established.

directions: Satinleaf is one of the most visually striking native trees of south Florida's tropical hardwood hammocks, native to coastal hammocks, marl prairies, and pinelands from Brevard and Hillsborough counties south through the Keys and into the Caribbean, recognized immediately by its distinctive two-toned leaves — rich, dark, glossy green on top and brilliant coppery-golden on the undersides — that shimmer and flash in the breeze with each passing gust. Small white flowers produce small, dark purple to black berries (1–1.5 cm) that are sweet and edible and an important food for mockingbirds, robins, and migrating warblers; the fruit is also edible for people, with a flavor reminiscent of a mild grape. In south Florida native landscapes it works beautifully as a specimen tree, understory tree under larger canopy, or informal screen in partial shade; it tolerates the thin, rocky, alkaline marl and limestone soils of Miami-Dade County where many plants struggle. Grow in well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full sun in zones 9b–11.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade. Marlberry is not to be confused with the invasive Ardisia crenata (Coral Ardisia) — Marlberry is a native species with smaller, less toothed leaves and larger black berries. Always verify you are purchasing the native species.

directions: Marlberry is a handsome native evergreen shrub or small tree, native to the coastal hammocks, tropical hardwood forests, and pinelands of south Florida and the Keys, growing as a common and important component of the hammock understory layer in partial to full shade. It produces clusters of small white flowers from summer through fall, followed by clusters of round, glossy black berries that are one of the most reliable and abundant bird food sources in south Florida's hammock ecosystem — the berries are consumed by dozens of species of warblers, thrushes, vireos, and resident birds throughout fall and winter. An adaptable and attractive native shade shrub for zone 9b–11 landscapes, Marlberry fills the role of native understory shrub in conditions too shaded for most flowering shrubs, providing year-round evergreen structure, summer-fall blooms, and exceptional wildlife value. Important note: always confirm you are purchasing native Marlberry (Ardisia escallonioides) and not the look-alike invasive Coral Ardisia (Ardisia crenata), which has escaped cultivation and invaded Florida's native hammocks.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in very well-drained, highly acidic (pH 3.5–5.5), dry, sandy soil in full sun. Florida Rosemary requires the harsh, fire-maintained, nutrient-poor scrub conditions to which it is adapted; it will decline in rich, moist, or shaded conditions.

directions: Florida Rosemary is one of the most ecologically fascinating native shrubs in North America — a dominant species of the imperiled Florida scrub habitat, native to the ancient, white sand ridges of the Florida peninsula where it creates one of the strangest visual phenomena in American botany: "Florida rosemary rings," circular patches of bare white sand 3–20 feet in diameter surrounding each plant, kept barren by allelopathic chemicals (terpenes and phenolics) that the plant releases through its roots to prevent competing plants from establishing. Not a true rosemary (Rosmarinus), it bears superficial resemblance to the culinary herb with its needle-like, aromatic, evergreen leaves densely arranged on erect stems, but is a monotypic genus in the heath family (Ericaceae). Small red-orange flowers in fall are followed by tiny berries consumed by Florida scrub-jays, gopher tortoises, and other scrub species. Grow in the driest, most infertile, most acidic sand you can provide in full sun; it is intolerant of irrigation, rich soil, shade, or the weed-suppressive conditions that most ornamental plants require.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring after all frost danger has passed, in rich, moist, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade. In zones 8–9, Abyssinian Banana must be treated as a large annual or overwintered indoors; dig and pot the plant before frost and store in a frost-free location.

directions: Abyssinian Banana is the most dramatic of the ornamental bananas, native to the highland forests of Ethiopia and East Africa and grown worldwide as a bold tropical accent plant for its colossal size and the extraordinary visual impact of its enormous leaves — reaching up to 10 feet long with striking red midribs on deep green blades — that create an instant tropical atmosphere wherever they are planted. Unlike true bananas (Musa species), Abyssinian Banana is monocarpic, meaning each trunk (pseudostem) flowers once after 3–10 years and then dies; unlike rhizomatous bananas it does not produce pups from the base, and in Ethiopia the starchy corm and pseudostem are fermented to produce enset (a culturally crucial food for over 20 million people) rather than relying on the fruit for food. In zones 9b–11 it persists as a perennial; in zones 8–9 it is typically grown as an impressive annual or dug and stored frost-free for the winter. Provide the richest, most moisture-retentive soil available, regular fertilization, and full sun for maximum leaf size.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in wet to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade. Pond Apple thrives in the permanently wet, flooded soils of swamp margins and mangrove edges where other fruit trees cannot grow; it is widely used as a rootstock for other Annona species such as soursop and atemoya.

directions: Pond Apple is a native small tree, native to the freshwater swamps, mangrove edges, bayheads, and tidal freshwater areas of south Florida and the Keys, with a natural range extending through the Caribbean and tropical Americas, where it grows with its roots often in permanently standing water — one of the few Annona relatives to tolerate prolonged flooding. The smooth, yellowish-green fruits (3–5 inches across) resemble a large apple or papaya and are edible but bland and insipid raw, though they are an important food for Florida alligators and various wildlife; they are used in the Everglades region to make a mildly fermented beverage. Pond Apple is widely used in tropical horticulture as a flood-tolerant rootstock for grafting soursop, atemoya, sugar apple, and other custard-apple family members, and its extreme waterlogging tolerance makes it invaluable for restoration planting in flooded south Florida ecosystems. Grow in wet, moist, or occasionally flooded conditions in full sun to partial shade in zones 9b–11.
